nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers and their backwaters; prefers areas with soft substrate, submerged rocks and driftwood providing daytime shelter; also found in marginal areas with floating vegetation

decorPreferences: large caves formed from driftwood tangles or stacked rock
multiple hiding spots to reduce daytime stress
floating or surface vegetation for subdued lighting
smooth or fine-sand substrate to protect barbels

diet.notes: Unfussy and robust feeder. Accepts sinking catfish pellets, high-quality wafers, freeze-dried and frozen foods (bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, chopped prawns), and vegetable matter (spirulina wafers, blanched courgette, peas). Feed at dusk or after lights-out to match nocturnal activity pattern. Small live foods and chopped earthworms eagerly taken. Plant matter should form a regular portion of the diet.

goodWith: larger West African tetras (Congo tetra, Distichodus spp.)
robust cichlids of similar size (Hemichromis, Chromidotilapia)
avoidWith: small fish that can be eaten (under ~5 cm)
other Synodontis species (territorial disputes at close quarters)
shy or slow-moving bottom dwellers competing for caves
any species sensitive to copper-based medications

summary: A robust West African squeaker catfish from the Niger basin and beyond, recognised by its bold ocellated (eye-spot) pattern of black-ringed pale spots. Grows to ~20 cm in aquaria, is strictly nocturnal, and audibly stridulates when handled. Intermediate care; copper-sensitive.
careNotes: The Ocellated Synodontis is one of the larger and more striking members of the Mochokidae family, distributed across the major river systems of West Africa including the Senegal, Niger, Volta, and Lake Chad basins. In aquaria the species typically attains 15–20 cm, though wild specimens can exceed 40 cm; aquarium-kept individuals rarely approach the wild maximum. A minimum 208 L (55 US gallon) tank with a footprint of at least 120 cm is recommended for a single adult, allowing adequate bottom territory. The substrate should be fine sand or smooth gravel to avoid abrading the sensitive barbels, and numerous substantial cave structures — built from driftwood tangles, stacked slate, or purpose-made ceramic hides — are essential. Floating plants or a dimly lit area help replicate the diffuse-light conditions the species prefers during daylight. This species is strictly nocturnal; it will spend the daytime hidden and become active at dusk. Feeding should be timed accordingly, offering sinking foods after lights-out. The Ocellated Synodontis is a "squeaker" (family Mochokidae): it produces an audible stridulation by rotating the spined pectoral girdle, particularly when handled or stressed. The pectoral-fin spines are strong and serrated — use a soft mesh net to avoid entanglement, which can injure both fish and handler. Like most catfish and scaleless fish, this species is copper-sensitive; copper-based medications (used for ich and velvet in community tanks) must never be used in the same system. Water quality should be maintained with good filtration and regular partial water changes; the species tolerates a broad pH and hardness range (pH 6.2–8.0, gH 5–25) and is forgiving of typical tap water in most regions. Slight territorial behaviour toward conspecifics and other Synodontis may emerge in mature adults; a single specimen per tank is usually the safest approach unless the aquarium is very large (300+ L) with abundant caves. Compatible with robust West African tankmates of similar size; any fish small enough to be swallowed will eventually be eaten.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium has not been reliably documented. Commercial farms produce Synodontis ocellifer using hormone-injection protocols to induce spawning. In nature, eggs are scattered with no parental care. The species reaches sexual maturity at approximately 12 cm. Hobbyist accounts of captive breeding are essentially absent; this species should be treated as "not in captivity" for practical breeding purposes.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis ocellifer
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided tank dimensions (90×30×30 cm, 80 L absolute minimum), temperature 23–28°C, pH 6.2–8.0, hardness 5–25°H, native range (Nigeria, Gambia, Chad, Cameroon, Senegal, Mali, Ghana, Niger, Guinea), habitat description, diet, temperament, compatible species, and breeding notes. Adult size stated as ~20 cm in aquaria (wild reports to ~60 cm noted but considered outliers for hobby purposes).

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis ocellifer
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), maximum TL (49 cm recorded), temperature (23–27°C), native range (Senegal, Gambia, Niger incl. Benue, Volta, Lake Chad, Ghana Densu, Nigeria Kwa Ibo), habitat (freshwater benthopelagic, potamodromous), IUCN Least Concern status. Trophic level 3.1 (carnivore-leaning omnivore in wild). FishBase max length 49 cm treated as wild maximum; aquarium adult size set to 15–20 cm per SeriouslyFish and hobbyist consensus.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Synodontis Ocellifer Catfish
additional.notes: Confirmed: adult size ~8" (20 cm) in trade, temperature 75–82°F (24–28°C), pH 6.5–7.8, dKH 4–15, minimum tank 50 gal (190 L), omnivore, peaceful temperament, bottom scavenger, requires caves and hiding places, captive breeding not achieved, pectoral-spine entanglement warning.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Ocellated Synodontis
additional.notes: Supplementary data: pH 6.5–7.8, temperature 23–27°C, hardness 4–15°N, lifespan 8–12 years, diet (sinking pellets, frozen/live foods, vegetable matter), swim zone (bottom, nocturnal), tank décor requirements, native range (Senegal, Gambia, Niger incl. Benue, Volta, Lake Chad). Minimum tank noted as 500–750 L for optimal adult housing — used 208 L (55 gal) as minimum per LiveAquaria and trade consensus; 500 L noted as optimal in careNotes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis species profiles
additional.notes: General Synodontis genus coverage used to confirm squeaker stridulation mechanism (pectoral-spine rotation against the cleithrum), copper sensitivity of scaleless catfishes, and nocturnal foraging behaviour. Species-specific page not definitively confirmed accessible at time of research; genus-level information cross-checked against SeriouslyFish and FishBase.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented hobbyist breeding. Commercial production relies on hormone-induced spawning. Eggs scattered, no parental care. Sexual maturity reached at approximately 12 cm TL.
